%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.5-5 Improper Rational Functions — The Special Case m = n

> **Section Objective:** Learn the fast, exam-safe method for handling an improper rational function when the numerator and denominator have exactly the same degree.

Consider F(x) = (3x² + 9x − 20) / (x² + x − 6). Both numerator and denominator are degree 2, so this is an improper fraction — but a very special one. The exam shortcut: **do not launch into full polynomial long division.** Instead, peel off a single constant equal to the ratio of the leading coefficients, then treat everything that remains exactly like an ordinary proper partial fraction problem. The cover-up method you already know applies without modification to the leftover terms. This one small adjustment — separating the constant first — converts what looks like a harder problem into a nearly routine cover-up exercise.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![For m = n, the textbook rewrites the improper rational function as a single constant (the leading-coefficient ratio) plus the usual sum of simple partial fractions.](/pages/page-034.png)

$$F(x)=\frac{b_nx^n+b_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+b_0}{x^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_0}=b_n+\frac{k_1}{x-\lambda_1}+\frac{k_2}{x-\lambda_2}+\cdots+\frac{k_n}{x-\lambda_n}$$
*When the denominator is monic and m = n, the only structural addition over the proper case is the leading constant b_n; every remaining coefficient k_r is still found by the standard cover-up substitution x = λ_r, exactly as before.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*📊 Left: the original same-degree rational function. Right: the immediate setup — constant first, then standard cover-up. The warning at the bottom flags the most common exam error: omitting the constant 3.*
![Chart](/generated/chart-1777145694018-9wkwl.png)

## 1. The Special Rule When m = n

When the numerator and denominator of a rational function have **exactly the same degree**, the function is improper — but only barely. For a monic denominator (leading coefficient 1), the rule is precise: the extra term you must add is the constant **b_n**, the leading coefficient of the numerator. Write it down first, before touching any partial fraction setup.

Once that constant is separated, the remaining expression is proper, and the cover-up formula

$$k_r = \bigl[(x - \lambda_r)\,F(x)\bigr]_{x=\lambda_r}$$

applies without any modification.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Students often assume that "improper" always demands full polynomial long division. **In the m = n case, long division is unnecessary** — the quotient is simply the constant b_n, and the remainder is already in the right form.

### KEY INSIGHT

Repeated roots and irreducible quadratic factors still follow the same procedures you learned for proper fractions — just apply them to the leftover proper part after the constant is separated. Nothing else changes.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Once the constant 3 is separated, the textbook computes k1 and k2 by the same root-substitution (cover-up) method used for ordinary proper partial fractions.](/pages/page-035.png)

## 2. Worked Example and High-Score Answer Framing

Let F(x) = (3x² + 9x − 20) / ((x − 2)(x + 3)).

**Step 1 — Write the setup immediately.**

The numerator and denominator are both degree 2, and the leading coefficient of the numerator is 3. So:

$$F(x) = 3 + \frac{k_1}{x-2} + \frac{k_2}{x+3}$$

**Step 2 — Cover-up for k₁.**

Multiply both sides by (x − 2) and set x = 2:

$$k_1 = \frac{3(4) + 9(2) - 20}{(2+3)} = \frac{12+18-20}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2$$

**Step 3 — Cover-up for k₂.**

Multiply both sides by (x + 3) and set x = −3:

$$k_2 = \frac{3(9) + 9(-3) - 20}{(-3-2)} = \frac{27-27-20}{-5} = \frac{-20}{-5} = 4$$

**Final answer:**

$$F(x) = 3 + \frac{2}{x-2} + \frac{4}{x+3}$$

### EXAM TIP

Strong exam answers always show the setup line (with the constant already separated) before computing any coefficient — this makes your method transparent and earns partial credit even if arithmetic slips occur. Keeping the setup step and the coefficient calculation visually distinct also prevents sign errors and makes your work far easier for a grader to follow.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- When m = n, separate the leading-coefficient constant first, then apply standard cover-up to the rest.
- Trap: forgetting the constant term produces an incomplete decomposition, even if all k values are correct.
- Repeated roots and quadratic factors still follow the usual proper-fraction procedures after the constant is removed.

*In the next section we will see a modified partial-fraction form used for inverse z-transform work.*
